title: Transcripts and other historical materials
summary: This source is a Federal Reserve navigation page describing the types of historical FOMC documents available for public access, including transcripts, minutes, Beige Books, Greenbooks, and policy statements. The page is a catalog/index that explains the structure and availability of these documents rather than containing the actual documents themselves. No specific economic data, policy decisions, or market-moving information is present in this source.
key_points: This is a container/navigation page for FOMC historical document types, not the documents themselves
Describes document categories: transcripts, minutes, Beige Books, Greenbooks (1964-2010), Bluebooks (1965-2010), Policy Statements, and Summary of Economic Projections
Most documents are released to the public with approximately a five-year lag
No specific economic figures, projections, or policy decisions are contained in this page
The page explains the history and format of each document type (e.g., Tealbook A and B replaced Greenbook and Bluebook in June 2010)

issues_found: Source is a navigation/container page cataloging FOMC document types, not the documents themselves
No specific economic data, policy decisions, or market-moving content present
No source URL provided in metadata, making document retrieval impossible
Container pages are explicitly non-archivable per Rule 8 and Rule 9
Zero archival value as standalone research note for macro catalysts

notes: This source is a low-quality container page from the Federal Reserve website. Per Rule 9, navigation pages and listing pages should be treated as non-archiveable. The page describes the existence and structure of FOMC historical documents (transcripts, minutes, Beige Books, etc.) but contains no actual document content, economic data, policy decisions, or macro catalyst information. There is no URL provided in the source metadata, making it impossible to retrieve the actual documents referenced. To be useful for a finance research archive focused on macro catalysts, this would need to be replaced with actual FOMC meeting transcripts, minutes, or policy statements that contain specific, source-grounded economic information.
